title: Cottage Pie
subtitle: Minced beef and vegetable filling under a creamy mashed-potato crust
excerpt: Cottage pie is a solid weeknight staple: browned beef mince cooked down with onion, carrot, and stock, then topped with mash and baked until golden. It freezes well and feeds a crowd.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: minced-collops-and-potato-crust entry. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845): beef mince and potato methods.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely, cover tightly, and freeze for up to 3 months. Thaw in the fridge overnight and bake from cold at 180°C for 40 minutes, covered with foil for the first 20.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the batch and freeze one portion before the baking stage.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble up to 24 hours ahead and refrigerate unbaked. Add 10 minutes to the oven time.

glossaryTerms.definition: Adding liquid to a hot pan after browning meat or vegetables to lift the caramelised bits stuck to the base. These stuck bits contain concentrated flavour and dissolve into the sauce.

content.text: Cottage pie is minced beef cooked down with onion, carrot, and stock into a savoury filling, then topped with mashed potato and baked until the surface browns. It takes about an hour start to finish. The filling can sit under the mash for a day in the fridge before baking, which makes it a useful dish to assemble when time allows and bake when you're ready.

Heat a large, wide pan over a high flame. Add the {{beef-mince}} of beef mince and break it up with a wooden spoon. Cook without stirring for 3 to 4 minutes until the base browns, then stir and continue cooking until no pink remains. Drain off any excess fat if needed.

Push the mince to one side. Add a little oil and soften the {{onion}} diced onions, {{carrot}} diced carrots, and {{celery}} sliced celery sticks over a medium heat for 8 minutes. Add the {{garlic}} of crushed garlic and cook for 1 minute more.
Stir in the {{tomato-puree}} of tomato puree and cook for 2 minutes.
with a splash of the stock, scraping the base. Add the remaining {{stock-beef}} of beef stock, the Worcestershire sauce, {{bay-leaves}} of bay, and dried thyme. Season well.
Simmer uncovered for 20 to 25 minutes until the filling is thick and the liquid has mostly reduced. Taste and adjust. Remove the bay leaves. Pour into a large baking dish and let cool slightly.
Boil the {{potato}} of peeled potato chunks in well-salted water for 15 to 20 minutes until completely tender. Drain and let the steam evaporate for 2 minutes before mashing.

Mash thoroughly with the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter and {{whole-milk}} of warm milk until smooth. Season well. Spoon the mash over the filling and spread it to the edges, then drag a fork across the surface to create ridges that will catch colour in the oven.
Bake at 190°C for 25 to 30 minutes until the top is golden brown and the filling is bubbling at the edges. Rest for 5 minutes before serving.

items.cause: The filling was too loose when the mash was added, or the mash was too soft to hold.
items.fix: Reduce the filling until it is thick enough to hold the mash without the two layers mixing. The mash should be stiff enough to spread, not poured.

items.cause: Oven not hot enough, or the mash surface was too smooth and wet.
items.fix: Raise the oven to 200°C for the last 10 minutes. Scoring the surface with a fork helps browning by increasing the surface area.

items.cause: Not enough reduction time, or too much stock was added.
items.fix: Return the filling to the hob and simmer uncovered for another 10 minutes before topping.

items.cause: Overworked, usually from using a food processor, or too much liquid added too fast.
items.fix: Use a masher or ricer; never a blender. Add warm milk gradually and stop when the texture is smooth but still holds its shape.

content.text: Cottage pie by that name appears in British cookbooks from the late 18th century, when the potato became cheap enough to use as a crust. The distinction between cottage pie (beef) and shepherd's pie (lamb) was not always strict in older sources; both names settled into common use by the mid-Victorian period. The dish was, and remains, a way to use leftover roast meat or fresh mince.
